---

## Binary Search Tree Insertion — Time Complexity

**Average case:** O(log n) — each comparison eliminates roughly half the remaining tree.

**Worst case:** O(n) — occurs when insertions arrive in sorted order, degenerating the tree into a linked list.

### BST Insert Pseudocode

```
function bst_insert(root, key):
    if root is NULL:
        return new Node(key)
    if key < root.key:
        root.left = bst_insert(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.key:
        root.right = bst_insert(root.right, key)
    return root
```

---

## Red-Black Tree: How It Maintains Balance

A red-black tree is a self-balancing BST that enforces **five invariants** to guarantee O(log n) height:

| # | Invariant |
|---|-----------|
| 1 | Every node is either **red** or **black** |
| 2 | The **root** is always **black** |
| 3 | Every **NULL leaf** (sentinel) is **black** |
| 4 | A **red** node cannot have a **red** child (no two consecutive reds) |
| 5 | Every path from a node to its descendant NULL leaves has the **same number of black nodes** (black-height) |

These properties guarantee that the longest path is at most **2× the shortest path**, bounding tree height to **O(log n)**.

### RB-Tree Insert Pseudocode

Insertion is a two-phase process: **standard BST insert**, then **fix-up**.

```
function rb_insert(tree, key):
    node = new Node(key)
    node.color = RED            // always insert as red

    // Phase 1: Standard BST insert
    bst_insert(tree.root, node)

    // Phase 2: Fix red-black violations
    rb_insert_fixup(tree, node)


function rb_insert_fixup(tree, z):
    while z.parent.color == RED:
        if z.parent == z.parent.parent.left:
            uncle = z.parent.parent.right

            if uncle.color == RED:
                // --- Case 1: Uncle is RED ---
                // Recolor parent, uncle, and grandparent
                z.parent.color       = BLACK
                uncle.color          = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                z = z.parent.parent          // move focus up

            else:
                if z == z.parent.right:
                    // --- Case 2: Uncle is BLACK, z is right child ---
                    z = z.parent
                    left_rotate(tree, z)     // transform to Case 3

                // --- Case 3: Uncle is BLACK, z is left child ---
                z.parent.color        = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                right_rotate(tree, z.parent.parent)

        else:
            // Mirror: swap "left" and "right" in all of the above

    tree.root.color = BLACK      // enforce invariant #2
```

### The Three Fix-Up Cases (Illustrated)

```
Case 1: Uncle is RED              Case 2: Uncle BLACK,       Case 3: Uncle BLACK,
  Recolor only, move up             z is inner child           z is outer child
                                    Rotate to Case 3           Rotate + recolor

      G(B)           G(R)              G(B)                       P(B)
     / \            / \               / \                        / \
   P(R) U(R) →   P(B) U(B)        P(R) U(B)    →            z(R) G(R)
   /              /                   \                              \
 z(R)           z(R)                 z(R)                           U(B)
                                  left_rotate(P)          right_rotate(G)
```

### Rotations — The Structural Tool

Rotations are O(1) pointer swaps that restructure locally without violating the BST ordering property:

```
function left_rotate(tree, x):
    y = x.right
    x.right = y.left          // adopt y's left subtree
    if y.left ≠ NULL:
        y.left.parent = x
    y.parent = x.parent       // y takes x's position
    if x.parent == NULL:
        tree.root = y
    else if x == x.parent.left:
        x.parent.left = y
    else:
        x.parent.right = y
    y.left = x                // x becomes y's left child
    x.parent = y
```

`right_rotate` is the symmetric mirror.

---

## Complexity Summary

| Operation | BST (worst) | BST (avg) | Red-Black Tree (guaranteed) |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|----------------------------|
| Insert    | O(n)        | O(log n)  | **O(log n)**               |
| Search    | O(n)        | O(log n)  | **O(log n)**               |
| Delete    | O(n)        | O(log n)  | **O(log n)**               |
| Fix-up    | —           | —         | **O(log n)** recolors + **≤ 2 rotations** |

The key insight: insertion fix-up does **at most 2 rotations** (O(1) structural changes) but may recolor up the full height of the tree (O(log n) recolorings). This makes red-black trees particularly efficient for insert-heavy workloads compared to AVL trees (which may rotate O(log n) times).
